input.prompt: A developer reports that CLI builds with 'dotnet build' are slow for incremental builds, even when nothing has changed. Visual Studio builds the same project almost instantly. Review the project and developer report, then suggest how to improve CLI build performance for the dev inner loop.

input.initialPrompt: A developer reports that CLI builds with 'dotnet build' are slow for incremental builds, even when nothing has changed. Visual Studio builds the same project almost instantly. Review the project and developer report, then suggest how to improve CLI build performance for the dev inner loop.

data.reasoningText: Let me review the project files and developer report to understand the issue.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
1. ## Developer Complaint
2. 
3. Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
4. for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
5. while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
6. 
7. We've already verified:
8. - No analyzers are slowing things down
9. - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
10. - The project itself compiles in <500ms
11. 
12. The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
13. project graph on every CLI invocation.
14. 
15. We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
16. command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.
17.

 ## Developer Complaint
 
 Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
 for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
 while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
 
 We've already verified:
 - No analyzers are slowing things down
 - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
 - The project itself compiles in <500ms
 
 The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
 project graph on every CLI invocation.
 
 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
 command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.

+++ b/tmp/sv-9a06755e7884487f839bbc8662472f0a/developer-report.md
@@ -1,17 +1,17 @@
 ## Developer Complaint
 
 Our developers report that running `dotnet build` from the terminal feels sluggish
 for incremental builds. Even when nothing has changed, CLI builds take 2-4 seconds,
 while the same project rebuilds nearly instantly inside Visual Studio.
 
 We've already verified:
 - No analyzers are slowing things down
 - Incremental build targets are working (targets do skip correctly)
 - The project itself compiles in <500ms
 
 The overhead seems to come from MSBuild process startup and re-evaluation of the
 project graph on every CLI invocation.
 
 We're looking for ways to speed up the developer inner loop when building from the
 command line, especially for quick edit-build-test cycles.

data.content:
## Analysis

The developer report confirms the bottleneck isn't compilation itself — it's **MSBuild process startup and project re-evaluation** on every `dotnet build` invocation. Visual Studio avoids this by keeping MSBuild loaded in-process via a long-running design-time build server.

## Recommendations

### 1. Use the MSBuild Server (built-in since .NET 8)

The biggest win. Add to your `.csproj` or a `Directory.Build.props`:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <MSBuildServerEnabled>true</MSBuildServerEnabled>
</PropertyGroup>
```

Or pass it on the command line:

```
dotnet build -p:MSBuildServerEnabled=true
```

This keeps an MSBuild worker process alive between invocations — eliminating JIT/startup overhead and keeping the project graph cached, just like VS does.

### 2. Use `dotnet watch` for edit-build loops

```
dotnet watch build
```

This monitors for file changes and rebuilds incrementally, avoiding repeated process spin-up entirely.

### 3. Enable Graph-based Restore Skip

```
dotnet build --no-restore
```

If you've already restored and haven't changed dependencies, skip the restore phase (which re-evaluates NuGet assets on every invocation by default).

### 4. Summary of expected gains

| Approach | Saves |
|----------|-------|
| `MSBuildServerEnabled` | ~1–2s (process startup + JIT) |
| `--no-restore` | ~0.5–1s (NuGet evaluation) |
| `dotnet watch` | Near-instant rebuilds |

The **MSBuild Server** is the closest equivalent to what Visual Studio does internally and directly addresses the reported 2–4s overhead.
